sections.title: Chinese Verbs Don't Conjugate
sections.explanation: Unlike European languages, Chinese verbs never change form. There are no conjugations for person, number, tense, or mood. The verb 吃 (chī, to eat) stays 吃 whether you say 'I eat', 'she eats', 'we ate', or 'they will eat'. Time is expressed through context, time words (昨天 yesterday, 明天 tomorrow), and aspect particles (了, 过, 着) — not by changing the verb itself. This makes Chinese verbs much simpler to learn: once you know a verb, you can use it in any situation.
sections.tip: Think of Chinese verbs as fixed building blocks. Instead of memorizing conjugation tables, focus on learning time words and particles that go around the verb.

examples.en: I eat. You eat. He eats. — same verb for all persons
I ate noodles yesterday. — 昨天 (yesterday) sets the time
She will go to Beijing tomorrow. — 明天 (tomorrow) sets the future
We are studying Chinese now. — 现在 (now) sets the present

exercises.prompt: How does the verb 吃 (chī) change for 'she eats' versus 'I eat'?
options: It doesn't change — 吃 stays the same
It adds a suffix for third person
It changes tone for different subjects
exercises.explanation: Chinese verbs never conjugate. 我吃 (I eat) and 她吃 (she eats) use exactly the same verb form.

exercises.prompt: How does Chinese indicate past tense in '我昨天去了学校' (I went to school yesterday)?
options: With the time word 昨天 and the particle 了
By changing 去 to a past form
By adding a prefix to the verb
exercises.explanation: Chinese uses time words like 昨天 (yesterday) and aspect particles like 了 to indicate when something happened, not verb conjugation.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'We will eat tomorrow'?
exercises.explanation: 明天 (tomorrow) already signals the future. The verb 吃 stays unchanged. 了 and 过 indicate completed actions, so they would contradict 'tomorrow'.

sections.title: Common Action Verbs
sections.explanation: Here are ten essential action verbs you will use every day: 吃 (chī, eat), 喝 (hē, drink), 看 (kàn, look/watch/read), 听 (tīng, listen), 说 (shuō, speak/say), 读 (dú, read aloud/study), 写 (xiě, write), 走 (zǒu, walk/leave), 跑 (pǎo, run), 坐 (zuò, sit). In Chinese, the basic sentence pattern is Subject + Verb + Object, the same as English. To negate a verb, put 不 (bù) before it: 我不吃 (I don't eat). For past negation, use 没 (méi): 我没吃 (I didn't eat).
sections.tip: 看 (kàn) is versatile — it can mean 'look at', 'watch' (TV), or 'read' (a book). Context tells you which meaning is intended.

examples.en: I eat an apple; he drinks tea.
She watches TV; I listen to music.
You speak Chinese; I write Chinese characters.
He doesn't run; he is sitting.

exercises.explanation: 听 (tīng) means 'to listen'. 说 means 'to speak/say' and 读 means 'to read aloud'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I don't drink coffee'?
exercises.explanation: 不 (bù) goes before the verb for general negation: 我不喝咖啡 = I don't drink coffee (as a habit). 没喝 would mean 'didn't drink' (a specific past event).
看书 means 'to read a book' (literally 'look-book'). 看 combined with 书 (book) gives the meaning of reading.

sections.title: Verb + Object Compounds
sections.explanation: Many common Chinese verbs are actually verb-object compounds: a verb paired with a default object. 吃饭 (chī fàn, eat-rice = eat a meal), 喝水 (hē shuǐ, drink-water = have a drink), 看书 (kàn shū, look-book = read), 说话 (shuō huà, speak-words = talk/speak), 走路 (zǒu lù, walk-road = walk). These are called separable verbs (离合词) because other elements can be inserted between the verb and object. For example, 吃了饭 (ate a meal) inserts 了 between 吃 and 饭. When you add a specific object, the default object is dropped: 吃饭 → 吃面条 (eat noodles), not 吃饭面条.
sections.tip: When you want to add a modifier like 了 or a duration, split the compound: 吃了饭, 说了两个小时的话. The default object drops when you supply a specific one.

examples.en: Have you eaten? (literally: eat-rice + 了 + question)
We walk to school. (literally: walk-road go school)

exercises.prompt: What is the literal meaning of 吃饭 (chī fàn)?
exercises.explanation: 吃饭 literally means 'eat rice' but is used generally to mean 'eat a meal' or 'have you eaten?' — rice being the staple food in Chinese culture.

exercises.prompt: If you want to say 'eat noodles', which is correct?
exercises.explanation: When adding a specific object, the default object 饭 is dropped. You say 吃面条, not 吃饭面条.

exercises.prompt: How do you insert 了 into 说话 to say 'spoke/talked'?
exercises.explanation: Separable verbs split to insert particles: 说 + 了 + 话 = 说了话 (spoke/talked). The particle goes between the verb and its object.

sections.title: 给 (gěi) and 让 (ràng)
sections.explanation: 给 (gěi) means 'to give' and is also used as a preposition meaning 'to/for someone'. Pattern: Subject + 给 + Person + Verb/Object. Example: 我给你一本书 (I give you a book). It can also mark the recipient of an action: 我给他打电话 (I make a phone call to him). 让 (ràng) means 'to let/allow/make (someone do something)'. Pattern: Subject + 让 + Person + Verb. Example: 妈妈让我学习 (Mom makes me study). 让 can express permission (let/allow) or causation (make/cause), depending on context.
sections.tip: 给 answers 'to/for whom?' while 让 answers 'who causes whom to do what?' Both place the affected person before the action verb.

examples.en: I give you a glass of water.
Please show it to me. (literally: give me a look)
Mom makes me go to bed early.
The teacher doesn't let us speak English.

exercises.prompt: What does '我给你打电话' mean?
options: I'll call you. (give you a phone call)
I let you make a call.
exercises.explanation: 给你 here means 'to you' — it marks the recipient of the action 打电话 (make a phone call). 我给你打电话 = I call you.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence means 'Mom lets me play'?
exercises.explanation: 让 + person + verb means 'let/allow someone to do something'. 妈妈让我玩 = Mom lets me play. 给 is for giving, and 和 means 'with'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Please give me a book'?
exercises.explanation: 给 + person + object means 'give someone something'. 请给我一本书 = Please give me a book. 让 would mean 'let/make', not 'give'.
